history: The client has repeatedly worked in environments characterized by micromanagement, which has become a recurring theme in their professional life. They report a pattern of self-blame specifically in work contexts. This suggests a longstanding vulnerability to internalizing negative workplace experiences as personal failings, likely reinforcing negative self-evaluations over time.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
intermediate_belief: If I am being micromanaged, it means I am not doing my job well enough. If I make a mistake or someone criticizes my work, it must be my fault. I have to be perfect at work or people will not trust me.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I keep getting micromanaged, I will never succeed or feel good about myself at work.
coping_strategies: The client considers resignation as a way to escape the distress of micromanagement, but this does not address the underlying self-blame. They may withdraw from colleagues or avoid tasks that could be criticized, reinforcing feelings of inadequacy and perpetuating the cycle.
situation: Experiencing micromanagement at the workplace and considering resigning.
auto_thought: I'm being micromanaged again—this must mean I'm not good enough at my job. No matter what I do, they'll never trust me to do things right.
behavior: I withdraw from colleagues and avoid tasks I think will be criticized. I spend time thinking about quitting instead of addressing the problem directly.
